gold_note:
CHIEF COMPLAINT

Evaluation of abnormal renal ultrasound with atrophic right kidney.

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Next patient Ashley James is a 14-year-old female, previously healthy, who is here for evaluation of abnormal renal ultrasound with atrophic right kidney.

The father details a history of the patient coming home sick one day with extreme right-sided abdominal pain. Her parents brought her for evaluation, and they treated her for a stomach virus and administered fluids prior to sending her home. The next day, the patient's pain was more severe. The patient returned to the doctor's office and an ultrasound and additional testing was performed which revealed left kidney hypertrophy. At that time, they were encouraged to follow-up with her primary care physician. The patient was seen briefly by her physician the next day, which was insignificant. Ashley was still experiencing stomach pain and vomiting, her father brought her to another facility in Jacksonville. At that location, the patient's family received information that her right kidney was nonfunctioning, atrophic, and smaller than her left kidney. Additionally, cysts on her kidneys, ovaries, and other locations were found. Her lymph nodes were swollen, and she had "a lot of infections". The patient was sent back to follow-up with her primary care physician. The patient was unable to follow back up with her primary care physician as he was still out of town. The patient came to Atlanta, remained here for 4 days, was evaluated and treated with subsequent improvement of her symptoms. She has not vomited since.

Prior to her current medical complaints, the patient was healthy. The etiology of her kidney issues is believed to have been caused by some severe infection the patient experienced, possibly as a child.

When the patient was 4 years old, she was hospitalized 3 or 4 times, for up to 2-3 days each time. She received fluids and antibiotics, and then discharged. Her father reports that no workup was ever performed to rule out infections during these hospitalizations. A week or 2 post-discharge the fever and symptoms would return. This kept recurring for approximately 2 months and then it spontaneously resolved.

The patient is still experiencing some stomach burning. She reports hesitancy while urinating and she states she has to wait for her urinary flow to commence. She endorses occasional dysuria. Ashley denies hematuria. Some beverages are associated with dysuria, thus the patient drinks mainly water, Gatorade and V8 juice.

SOCIAL HISTORY

The patient plays on softball team and is interested in joining a drill team over the summer.

FAMILY HISTORY

Paternal Grandfather: Deceased approximately 47. Myocardial Infarction. Posterior Urethral Valves. Obstructive Neuropathy. End Stage Chronic Kidney Disease was Dialysis dependent. Failed Renal Transplant.
Paternal Great Aunt: End Stage Chronic Kidney Disease is Dialysis dependent. Type II Diabetes. Hypertension.

No family history of polycystic kidney disease.

CURRENT MEDICATIONS

Pepcid 40 mg once daily.
Claritin as needed for allergies.

PHYSICAL EXAM

Constitutional
Well-appearing female adolescent, sitting comfortably on the exam table in no acute distress.

Neurological
Alert.

RESULTS

Abdominal ultrasound, 03/22/2021.
CT abdomen and pelvis, 03/22/2021.
CMP, 03/22/2021.

ASSESSMENT

 Renal hypoplasia.
Ashley is a 14-year-old female who presents today for evaluation of abnormal renal ultrasound with atrophic right kidney. She has right-sided renal hypoplasia with a small atrophic right kidney discovered incidentally on imaging for abdominal pain. Imaging shows compensatory hypertrophy of her left kidney. Otherwise, she shows no sequelae of chronic kidney disease and enjoys normal blood pressure. Her urinalysis is without sediment. She has normal kidney function with creatinine of 0.62, which equates to an estimated GFR of greater than 90 mL/min per 1.73 m2, and normal serum electrolytes and acid base status. Ashley is at higher risk of hypertension and proteinuria, which will need to be followed annually.

PLAN

Yearly ultrasound to monitor the hypertrophic left kidney and atrophic right kidney. Also, on an annual basis I recommend completion of a urinalysis to assess for proteinuria and blood pressure checks. The patient should avoid use of nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory medications and use Tylenol for pain and fever. It is also recommended that she stay well hydrated, avoiding dehydration, especially while participating in sports, by consuming 2 L of water per day. I counseled the patient and family about the importance of a healthy diet and remaining active to avoid future comorbidities that may put her at increased risk for chronic kidney disease as in adult.

The patient does not need to follow up with a pediatric nephrologist but can instead follow up with her primary care physician or an adult nephrologist closer to home.

INSTRUCTIONS

Schedule follow up appointment with local adult nephrologist or primary care physician.
notes:
Subjective:
HPI: Ashley James, a 14-year-old female, presents with a history of abdominal pain and vomiting, initially thought to be appendicitis, but later found to have an atrophic right kidney on ultrasound. She has a history of recurrent infections as a child, with hospitalizations at 4 years old for 3-4 times, with symptoms of fever and vomiting, but no specific diagnosis made. She has been taking Pepcid 40mg daily for symptoms and Claritin for allergies. Her family history includes renal disease, with her father having a kidney transplant, aunt with end-stage renal disease, and mother with breast cancer. Ashley's father died at 47 due to a massive heart attack while on dialysis.

Past medical history: Ashley has a history of recurrent infections as a child, with hospitalizations at 4 years old for 3-4 times, with symptoms of fever and vomiting. Her father had a kidney transplant and died at 47 due to a massive heart attack while on dialysis. Her aunt has end-stage renal disease, chronic kidney disease, stage V, diabetes, and hypertension. Ashley's mother has breast cancer.

Review of systems: Ashley complains of abdominal pain, vomiting, and burning sensation when urinating, especially after consuming certain drinks like soda.

Current medications: Pepcid 40mg daily, Claritin for allergies.

Objective:
Vital signs: [NOT MENTIONED]

Physical exam: Ashley is a well-appearing female, alert and oriented, with no acute distress. Her physical exam is within normal limits.

Test Results: [NOT MENTIONED]

Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: Ashley has a history of recurrent infections and an atrophic right kidney, with a family history of renal disease.

Problem list: Atrophic right kidney; Recurrent infections; End-stage renal disease; Chronic kidney disease; Stage V; Diabetes; Hypertension.

Plan:
I recommended that Ashley undergo an annual ultrasound to monitor the size of her kidneys and their overall function. I also suggested completing a urinalysis to ensure that protein and blood are not present in her urine, and a blood pressure check. I advised avoiding nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory medicines like ibuprofen and opting for Tylenol instead. I also recommended that Ashley stay well-hydrated, drinking at least 2L of water per day, and maintain a healthy diet to support her kidney health.

Follow-up:
Ashley can schedule an appointment with her primary care physician or an adult nephrologist closer to her home, rather than traveling to Atlanta.

Subjective:

She was previously healthy but recently experienced severe abdominal pain and was diagnosed with atrophic right kidney and cysts on her kidney and ovaries, along with swollen lymph nodes and infection. She has a history of recurrent infections, starting at 4 years old, with symptoms of fever and vomiting, but no underlying cause was identified. Her father had a kidney transplant due to obstructive neuropathy and died at 47 due to complications with dialysis. Her aunt has end-stage renal disease, chronic kidney disease, stage V, diabetes, and hypertension.

Her aunt has end-stage renal disease, chronic kidney disease, stage V, diabetes, and hypertension.

Plan:
I recommended that Ashley have an annual ultrasound to monitor the size of her kidneys and their overall function, complete a urinalysis to ensure protein and blood are not present in her urine, and have a blood pressure check. I advised her to avoid nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory medicines like ibuprofen and use Tylenol instead. I also recommended that she stay hydrated by drinking at least 2 liters of water per day, maintain a healthy diet, and continue her active lifestyle to help prevent kidney damage.

Subjective:

She was previously healthy but has been experiencing abdominal pain and nausea, which has led to multiple hospitalizations and diagnoses of a stomach virus and atrophic right kidney. Her symptoms have been ongoing for several months, with periods of remission and exacerbation. She has been taking Pepcid 40 mg daily and Claritin for allergies. She has a family history of renal disease, with her father having a kidney transplant due to obstructive neuropathy and end-stage renal disease. Her aunt also has end-stage renal disease, chronic kidney disease, stage V, diabetes, and hypertension.

Her father had posterior urethral valves and obstructive neuropathy, leading to chronic kidney disease, dialysis dependence, and a renal transplant that subsequently failed. He died in his 40s due to complications from dialysis.

Current medications: Pepcid 40 mg daily, Claritin.

Plan:

I recommended an annual ultrasound to monitor the size of her kidneys and their overall function, a urinalysis to ensure the absence of protein and blood in her urine, and regular blood pressure checks. I also emphasized the importance of staying hydrated, with a goal of drinking at least 2 liters of water per day, and maintaining a healthy diet and active lifestyle.

Subjective:
HPI: Ashley, a 14-year-old female, presents for evaluation of abnormal renal ultrasound with an atrophic right kidney. She reports a history of abdominal pain, particularly on the right side, which began suddenly and worsened over the next day. She was initially treated for a stomach virus but continued to experience pain and vomiting. Subsequent ultrasound revealed an atrophic right kidney. She has also had a history of recurring fevers and abdominal pain as a child, starting at the age of 4, with multiple hospitalizations for fluid and antibiotic treatment. Her mother suspects that these episodes may have contributed to her current kidney issues.

Past medical history: Ashley has a family history of kidney disease, with her father having a kidney transplant and end-stage renal disease, her aunt having end-stage renal disease, and her grandfather having posterior urethral valves, obstructive neuropathy, and chronic kidney disease. She is currently taking 40 mg of Pepcid daily and 40 mg of Claritin for allergies.

Review of systems: Ashley reports abdominal pain, particularly on the right side, and burning sensations when urinating, especially after consuming certain drinks. She also mentions occasional difficulty starting to urinate.

Current medications: Pepcid 40 mg daily, Claritin 40 mg daily.

Objective:
Vital signs: [NOT MENTIONED]
Physical exam: Ashley is a well-appearing female, alert and oriented, with no acute distress. Her physical exam is otherwise within normal limits.

Test Results: [NOT MENTIONED]

Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: Ashley has a history of abnormal renal ultrasound with an atrophic right kidney, likely due to a previous severe infection that may have affected her kidney function.

Problem list: Atrophic right kidney; Kidney disease; Family history of kidney disease; Abdominal pain; Difficulty starting to urinate; Burning sensations when urinating.

Plan:
I recommended that Ashley have an ultrasound to monitor the size of her kidneys and their overall function annually. I also suggested completing a urinalysis to ensure there is no protein or blood in her urine and performing a blood pressure check regularly. I advised avoiding nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory medications like ibuprofen and opting for Tylenol instead. Additionally, I recommended staying hydrated by drinking at least 2 L of water per day, maintaining a healthy diet, and continuing her active lifestyle.

Follow-up:
Ashley can either meet with her primary care physician or an adult nephrologist closer to her home for her next appointment.
